title: ALP, Coalition line up to deny teenage super
full_text:
Labor has defied calls from the ACTU and the Greens to implement superannuation for teenagers as part of its payday super legislation, after voting down a motion in the Senate on Wednesday.
Despite Jim Chalmers on Tuesday saying he wanted to engage young people and unions on the “important issue” of super for minors who work fewer than 30 hours a week, Labor and the Liberals voted against the move.
The government’s payday super bill, which became law on Wednesday, contains regulations that exempt businesses from having to pay super to some 515,000 Australians under 18 who work fewer than 30 hours a week. The exemptions are contained in regulations controlled by ministerial discretion.
The Greens put a disallowance motion to stop such ministerial discretion, but were knocked back. They will attempt their motion again next month. Greens finance spokeswoman Barbara Pocock said Labor and the Liberals were robbing young people: “Labor and the Coalition teamed up … to vote against teenage workers getting super and to pump the profits of Coles, Woolies and Maccas.”
